items.q: How does an oxbow lake form from a meander?
items.correct: Erosion on outside bends narrows the meander neck; during a flood the river cuts across the neck, deposition seals the old loop, leaving a horseshoe-shaped lake.
items.misconception: Oxbow lakes form when meanders dry up completely.
items.random_wrong: Oxbow lakes form where two rivers meet and one stops flowing.
items.why: The lake remains water-filled — it's a cut-off loop, not a dry feature. Students miss that the active river abandons the meander after cutting through, and deposition isolates the old channel.

items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: What is hydraulic action in a river?
items.correct: The force of moving water itself compressing air into cracks in the river bank or bed, weakening rock until pieces break off — no sediment needed.
items.misconception: Hydraulic action is when rocks in the river smash into each other.
items.random_wrong: Hydraulic action is when the river dissolves rocks chemically.
items.why: Students mix up the four river erosion processes. Hydraulic action = water force; abrasion = water using rocks to scrape; attrition = rocks hitting rocks; solution (corrosion) = chemical dissolving.

items.q: What is the highest peak in England, located in the Lake District?
items.why: It's Scafell Pike. “Snowdon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a SUSTAINABLE solution to Lake District traffic problems?
items.why: Correct answer: Park-and-ride schemes. “Wider roads through the park” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many MILLION visitors does the Lake District receive each year?
items.why: Exact answer: 18. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
